other_observers: Rachel Hopper, Nick Moore, and others; college student (don't know name) first alerted us to a bird with unstreaked undertail perched in tree near us. Bird then flew to feeder area where 6 of us studied it.
specific_location: Discovery Museum bird feeders and trees above the trail running behind the museum.
description: A redpoll species by the red "poll" on the bird's forecrown. Undertail coverts white with one long dark shaft streak on the longest feather. Belly white. Flanks with faint grayish streaks. Face with whitish wash to background and with a few darker feathers but no contrast between ear coverts and face = NOT a brownish face. Bill short, thin and yellow, surrounded above and below by SMALL AREA of BUSHY dark feathers hiding the base of the bill. Rump, when seen, did not have streaks to it (see photo by Nick Moore). Feet and legs black. Amount of red on forecrown smaller in length than COREs. Wings dark with two white wingbars. Back streaked brown and white. Looked for white scapulars but not sure about that feature. Overall frosty or cold appearance and noticeably lighter than even light-colored COREs.Behaviors: Traveled with large flock of redpolls, most identified as Commons. Came to feaders, thistle, platform, and millet feeder prefering it seemed the platform feeder and the perches above both sets of feeders. Fed voraciously. Perched for up to a minute, sometimes by itself.Call: flight calls not distinguised from much more abundant Common Redpolls who gave a type of chew chew chew in flightPlumage: basic - I think a first year male
photographer: Photographer: bill maynard, bill maynard, Nick Moore, Rachel Hopper
